parents.mother_name: Martha Salisbury Woolsey (1812-1852, died 12 days after Theodore's birth)
parents.mother_research_notes: His mother died only 12 days after his birth from complications. His father Theodore Dwight Woolsey remarried Sarah Sears Prichard two years later in 1854.

education.notes: Yale 1872 — initiate of Skull and Bones senior society same year.

career.notes: 33 years as Yale's Professor of International Law. Retired 1911.
One of the founders of the Yale Review and a frequent contributor.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1872 with Skull and Bones, then Law
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1872 — the same year he was initiated into Skull and Bones — and took his Bachelor of Laws at Yale Law School in 1876. He traveled in Europe before becoming Instructor in Public Law at Yale in 1877.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-three years as Yale International Law professor
narrative_blocks.body: From 1878 to 1911 he held Yale's Professor of International Law chair — thirty-three years. As his memorialist Charles Cheney Hyde wrote, for the first twenty years 'interest in the law of nations was not widespread or intense in America' and Woolsey 'was doing spade work, preparing the ground in his own land for the seeds there later sown.'

narrative_blocks.header: Edited father's textbook, founded Yale Review
narrative_blocks.body: In 1891 he published the sixth edition of his father's influential 'An Introduction to the Study of International Law' — reprinted 1901 and 1906. He was a founder of the Yale Review journal and authored 'America's Foreign Policy' (1898).

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died twelve days after his birth
narrative_blocks.body: Born to Yale president Theodore Dwight Woolsey and Martha Salisbury Woolsey on 22 October 1852, he lost his mother on 3 November 1852 — only twelve days later. His father remarried Sarah Sears Prichard two years afterward and had four more children with her.

sources.label: Charles Cheney Hyde 1929 memorial — American Journal of International Law

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Forest Service forester, WWI Major in the

career.notes: WWI commission as Major. The 10th Engineers were organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces; his duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use.
Post-WWI returned to CT as consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer; worked with national forestry organizations and conservation groups.

military_service.notes: Forestry timber procurement for AEF in France

narrative_blocks.header: WWI Major procuring timber in France for AEF
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he was commissioned Major in the 10th Engineers (Forestry) — a unit organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces. His duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use; he served until 1919 then returned to Connecticut as a consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer.

narrative_blocks.header: Author of American Forest Regulation textbook
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale forestry lecturer and consulting forester, he wrote 'American Forest Regulation' along with other books and articles on forestry and forest regulation. His work bridged the practical forestry of the U.S. Forest Service and the academic forestry program Yale pioneered.

narrative_blocks.header: Mount Woolsey named for him after death
narrative_blocks.body: He died by self-inflicted gunshot on 10 July 1933 at fifty-three. Days later, his daughter Betty Woolsey was on a climbing party in Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains; on hearing of his death she left for New Haven, and the remaining four climbers named the unclimbed peak they then summited Mount Woolsey in his memory. The name was formally approved in 1961.

burial.plot_notes: Trail Creek Ranch, Wilson WY — memorial service held at the ranch the summer after her death

education.school_name: Educated by French governess (no formal early school)
education.notes: Family travels in California, France, Switzerland, Italy with French governess.
Vassar after family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: U.S. National Downhill Champion (Mt Rainier WA)
career.notes: Won as captain of the American women's team.

career.role: Captain, U.S. Women's Olympic Ski Team
career.notes: First Winter Olympics to feature alpine skiing. Led 6-woman U.S. team in women's combined; finished 19th of 29 with 69.24 points and was leading U.S. finisher. Later described as 'Nazi Olympics.'

career.role: U.S. National Downhill Champion (Mt Hood OR)
First editor of Ski Illustrated magazine

career.organization: Trail Creek Ranch (dude ranch + ski center)
career.notes: Bought Trail Creek Ranch 1942; opened as ski center + dude ranch 1946. Pioneered recreational alpine skiing on Teton Pass shuttling enthusiasts up + leading powder descents. Established Jackson Hole's first Nordic ski track. Base for Olympic biathlon tryouts and Junior Nordic National Championships.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece Elizabeth Woolsey La Farge bearing the same given name
Distant cousin who won the Women's Western Golf Association four years running

narrative_blocks.header: Captained U.S. women's ski team, 1936 Olympics
narrative_blocks.body: She captained the inaugural U.S. women's Olympic ski team at the 1936 Winter Olympics in Garmisch-Partenkirchen Germany — the first Games to feature alpine skiing events. She led 6 American women in the women's combined and finished 19th of 29 as leading U.S. finisher; later she described the experience as skiing in the 'Nazi Olympics.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two national downhill championships, five years apart
narrative_blocks.body: She won the US National Downhill Championship at Mount Rainier WA in 1934 and again at Mount Hood OR in 1939 — bracketing her 1936 Olympic captaincy. Between them she joined the inaugural US Women's FIS Team at Mürren Switzerland 1935, racing world championships against elite European fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Bighorn peaks, climbing day father died
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1933 she was climbing in Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains with William and Alan Willcox when news arrived of her father's death; she left at once for New Haven. The four remaining climbers made the first ascent of an unclimbed peak and named it Mount Woolsey in her father's memory.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Trail Creek Ranch in Jackson Hole, 1942
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with resort skiing she settled at Jackson Hole WY in 1942, buying Trail Creek Ranch at the base of Teton Pass and opening it as a dude ranch + ski center by 1946. She pioneered recreational alpine skiing on Teton Pass and established the valley's first Nordic ski track — the seed of Jackson Hole Ski Club's Nordic program.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Ski Illustrated, wrote Off the Beaten Track
narrative_blocks.body: During NYC winters she served as the first editor of Ski Illustrated magazine 1943-1946, contributing to the sport's media growth in the U.S. Her 1984 autobiography 'Off the Beaten Track' documented her European Alps climbs, Olympic experience, and Jackson Hole ranching life.

marriages.spouse_name: Edward Hooper LaFarge (1901-1981), artist grandson of John LaFarge; RISD professor 30 yrs; m. 12 Jun 1935 Foxon East Haven CT; 7 children — Edward T, Natalie Goss, Anne Culman, Patricia Knopp, Elizabeth LaFarge, Margaret Pinsky, Catherine England

birth.country: Switzerland or France (per Wikipedia: 2 daughters born abroad)

narrative_blocks.header: Married at Center Church by Yale theology father
narrative_blocks.body: On 3 June 1909 he married Dorothy Buckingham Bacon of Lyme CT at Center Church New Haven — the same Center Church his Hooker forefathers had attended. The ceremony was performed by the bride's father Professor Benjamin W. Bacon (Yale Divinity), assisted by Dr. Endicott Peabody president of Groton School where Heathcote had been schooled.

cross_connections.notes: Woolsey Hall at Yale named for him; golden-toed statue on Old Campus.

narrative_blocks.header: 9th President of Yale
narrative_blocks.detail: Theodore Dwight Woolsey was elected to Yale's presidency in 1846, succeeding Jeremiah Day — the only person to serve between Woolsey's uncle Timothy Dwight IV (8th president, 1795-1817) and himself. His cousin once removed Timothy Dwight V would become the 12th president in 1886. Under Woolsey's 25-year presidency, Yale's Scientific School and School of Fine Arts were founded. He was widely published in international law and political economy, chaired the American commission for the revision of the Authorized Bible, and was president of the World's Evangelical Alliance at its international New York meeting.
narrative_blocks.body: Led Yale 1846-1871. His mother Elizabeth Dwight was sister of Yale's 8th president; his cousin once removed Timothy Dwight V was 12th president. Only Jeremiah Day served between the two family members.

narrative_blocks.header: Woolsey Hall and the golden-toed statue
narrative_blocks.detail: Woolsey Hall, completed in 1901, is one of Yale's most prominent buildings. Woolsey Street in New Haven also bears his name. The statue erected in his memory on Yale's Old Campus has acquired a golden toe from being rubbed for good luck by Yale students across more than a century. Theodore Dwight Woolsey is one of only three Yale presidents from the Hooker-descended Dwight family — the others being his uncle Timothy Dwight IV and his cousin once removed Timothy Dwight V.
narrative_blocks.body: Woolsey Hall at Yale (completed 1901) is named for him, as is Woolsey Street in New Haven. A bronze statue on Yale's Old Campus has a golden toe — rubbed for good luck by generations of students.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1820 valedictorian, then Greek across Europe
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at thirteen and graduated valedictorian in 1820, then spent a year of legal study in Philadelphia and two years of theology at Princeton. After tutoring at Yale he went abroad to study Greek at Leipzig, Bonn, and Berlin — preparation for the Greek professorship he held from 1831 to 1846.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years as Yale's tenth president
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the 10th President of Yale College from 1846 to 1871. Under his tenure Yale advanced in wealth and influence, and two new departments — the Sheffield Scientific School and the School of Fine Arts — were established. He personally instructed students of history, political economy, political science, and international law.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost six children in epidemics within nine years
narrative_blocks.body: Of his 13 children across two marriages, six died young: three sons and a daughter in the 1843-1854 scarlet fever and other epidemics, and two more daughters from typhoid fever in 1861. His statue on Yale's Old Campus has a golden toe from generations of students rubbing it for luck.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister of one Yale president, mother of another
narrative_blocks.body: She was Pope of Yale Timothy Dwight IV's younger sister and married William Walton Woolsey in Greenfield CT in April 1792. Her son Theodore Dwight Woolsey would become the 10th President of Yale (1846-1871) — making her the only person in Yale's history to be both sister to one Yale president and mother to another.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.

William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST068.name: Brown University
INST068.notes:
Co-founded 1764 as the College of Rhode Island. Ezra Stiles (HD0346) was among its founders — not the sole founder. Renamed Brown University 1804.
